title: Drilling and Debt
abstract: This paper documents a previously unrecognized debt-related investment distortion. Using detailed project-level data for 69 firms in the oil and gas industry, we find that highly levered firms pull forward investment, completing projects early at the expense of long-run project returns and project value. This behavior is particularly pronounced prior to debt renegotiations. We test several channels that could explain this behavior and find evidence consistent with equity holders sacrificing long-run project returns to enhance collateral values and, by extension, mitigate lending frictions at debt renegotiations.

article.introduction:
Understanding how debt affectsa firm's investment decisions is one of the central questions in finance. Incentive conflicts between debt and equity claimants have the potential to result in inefficient and value-destroying decisions (Jensen and Meckling (1976)). Existing theoretical and empirical work has focused largely on the size, prevalence, and mitigation of investment distortions linked with the traditional agency costs of debt such as underinvestment and risk-shifting.1
In this paper, we use new detailed project-level data to document a previously unrecognized debt-related investment distortion. We show that high-leverage firms facing debt renegotiations pull investment forward and complete projects early at the expense of project net present value (NPV). These negative project-level investment distortions likely aim to enhance collateral value, thereby mitigating lending frictions and increasing firms’ access to finance. Overall, our results suggest that by increasing collateral value, high-leverage firms (dependent on asset-based loans) mitigate financing frictions that arise at debt renegotiations. This finding also highlights a previously unexplored hidden cost of collateral-based financing.
Identifying how debt affects firms’ actions is empirically challenging. First, it is difficult to observe actions at the project level and identify how these actions affect firms’ cash flows. Second, even if one can observe managers’ detailed actions, assessing whether a decision is value maximizing requires that a clear, unambiguous counterfactual decision exists and that its value be observable. Third, leverage and the composition of credit agreements are not randomly assigned, and omitted endogenous variables could be related to both firm-level investment decisions and leverage, making it difficult to infer a causal relationship. Finally, the distortive effects of debt tend to arise only in the presence of market frictions that intensify as leverage increases (see, e.g., Stein (2003)).2
We exploit an empirical setting that allows us to make significant progress on the above challenges. Specifically, we use detailed project-level completion decisions from North American shale oil drilling projects to examine how oil and gas companies with different levels of leverage react to the severe contango episode that began in December 2014. This setting has several advantages.
First, we observe high-frequencyproject-levelcompany decisions and can quantify the effects of completing an individual oil well versus delaying completion of the well. Our data set contains detailed project-level data on 3,557 North American shale oil wells operated by 69 publicly traded oil and gas firms. We know the date of well spudding (project start) and well completion (first production and project cash flow), as well as the precise location of the well.
Second, the 2014 to 2015 contango episode offers a clear instance in which completion should be delayed. During this period, oil spot prices were significantly lower than oil futures prices. In February 2015, for example, six-month oil futures prices were 11% higher than spot prices (Figure1). The futures curve provides us with a clear counterfactual as it allows us to estimate the value of delaying completion. We show that a decision to accelerate well completion and to start oil production early is not value maximizing at the project level.3
Third, the detailed project-level data allow us to make progress in controlling for the endogeneity of potential differences ininvestment opportunitiesacross high-leverage and low-leverage firms (Lang, Ofek, and Stulz (1996), Ahn, Denis, and Denis (2006)). Using precise well location data, we control for differences in investment opportunities (well/project quality) via a set of granular township (six-by-six-mile) geography fixed effects.
Finally, we exploit the fact that a subsample of oil and gas firms secure debt via asset-based credit agreements (commonly referred to as asset-based lending, or ABL), which are characterized by seasonal credit renegotiations that expose our sample firms to credit market frictions. Theseprescheduledrenegotiations are not a consequence of covenant violations, distress, or default, but rather are a standard part of the debt contract lifecycle that are defined in advance. The timing of credit renegotiations and related exposure to lending frictions is thus plausibly exogenous with respect to the oil price contango period. We exploit the staggered timing of ABL renegotiations to assess the role that ABL and leverage may have on project completion. In our sample, 45 of 69 firms are subject to such credit agreement renegotiations, including all but one high-leverage firm (those in the top quintile of the leverage distribution).
The renegotiations and the contango period serve as the foundation of two separate difference-in-differences (DiD) analyses. While we do not offer an instrument for leverage, the combination of two different DiD empirical designs and granular geographic fixed effects imposes significant hurdles on nondebt-related interpretations of our results.
In comparing well completion decisions before and during contango, we find that on average high-leverage firms start producing 1.0 months earlier during contango, resulting in a 4.8% loss in project NPV, or $124,000 per project.4This effect is nonlinear and is concentrated among high-leverage firms (consistent with Gilje (2016), Chava and Roberts (2008), Bharath and Shumway (2008), who document other debt-related investment effects). The documented effect cannot be explained by firm heterogeneity in profitability, size, and market-to-book. More importantly, it cannot be fully explained by the mere presence of ABL: The effect existswithinthe sample of ABL firms, indicating that leverage plays an important role in the investment distortion. For an average firm, the aggregate effect of accelerating investments across the projects we focus on in this study is equivalent to a deadweight loss of about 1.2% of equity value.
The results of the second DiD analysis, which focuses on ABL renegotiations, yield similar conclusions: In the month prior to a prescheduled renegotiation or amendment, high-leverage firms complete 145% more wells than during the month following renegotiation. This number is compared to 63% for low-leverage firms. Furthermore, high-leverage firms exposed to debt renegotiations complete more wells prior to the renegotiation month than they complete during the month following renegotiation, relative to otherhigh-leverage firmsthat are not exposed to renegotiations and related market frictions. The latter result indicates that lending frictions (at renegotiation) play an important role in linking high leverage and investment distortions.
Although the notion that debt affects firms’ investment decisions has been prominently featured in the finance literature, the evidence we report is inconsistent with traditional distortions such as asset substitution (see, e.g., Gilje (2016) for an overview) or debt-overhang-related underinvestment (see, e.g., Melzer (2017) for an overview). Our results point to a previously unrecognized debt-related distortion as the documented deadweight loss stems fromacceleration(notdelay) of investments.
The short-term transparent nature of the decision to accelerate production in relation to debt renegotiations limits the number of potential economic forces capable of explaining the results. At each renegotiation, firms in our sample are effectively refinancing existing asset-based credit agreements, which expose them to traditional lending frictions that stem from asymmetric information and moral hazard (Aghion and Bolton (1992), Stulz and Johnson (1985), Hart and Moore (1998)). Covenants and collateral are prominently featured in prior literature as a mechanism that mitigates lending frictions ex ante (at the point of debt origination), and as a result expands access to, and lowers the cost of, debt.5Yet both can intensify frictions ex post. Covenant violations lead to significant reductions in firm investment (Chava and Roberts (2008), Nini, Smith, and Sufi (2009)) and curtail the availability of bank lines of credit (Sufi (2009)). Declines in collateral value can lead to smaller investments (Chaney, Sraer, and Thesmar (2012)).
In the second half of the paper, we evaluate whether our results can be driven by market frictions linked with (i) binding covenants and related immediate cash flow needs, and (ii) collateral constraints. First, we hypothesize that when facing binding cash flow or covenant constraints, firms distort their real decisions in an attempt to accelerate cash flows, as firms facing binding covenant restrictions benefit from access to immediate cash (Faulkender and Wang (2006)) and earnings management (Dichev and Skinner (2002)). We find that the project-level cash flow patterns and the backward-looking nature of the covenant metrics (almost mechanically) rule out binding-covenant-based explanations.6The acceleration of well completions should have a negative effect on a firm's immediate cash availability, as it takes 20 months for new production-related EBITDA to cover the $3.5 million CAPEX associated with a well completion. Furthermore, the detailed data on liquidity ratios and borrowing availability suggest that despite firms having high leverage, their current ratios, interest coverage ratios, and credit facility availability do not signal impending liquidity challenges at the time of credit renegotiations.
Second, we hypothesize that high-leverage firms forgo higher NPV projects to mitigate lending frictions and expand their access to finance by expanding their collateral base. Although collateral mitigates lending frictions at the point of debt origination, collateral constraints (similar to covenants) have the potential to expose borrowers to additional frictions at the point of debt renegotiations. This effect should be more pronounced for high-leverage firms and firms more dependent on asset-based borrowing (Berger and Udell (1990)). In our setting, well completions directly affect the collateral values ascribed by lenders and in turn a firm's ability to secure/expand access to finance.
To test this channel, we exploit two types of cross-sectional heterogeneity across individual oil wells in terms of their ability to add to a firm's collateral. First, we show that prior to debt renegotiations, high-leverage firms complete wells that have 43% higher daily production relative to those completed by the same firms after debt renegotiations. Second, we exploit the fact that newly completed wells offer a verifiable signal about the value of neighboringundevelopedoil reserves. The geographic proximity of undeveloped reserves to producing well(s) reduces asymmetric information and allows lenders to assign higher collateral values to even undrilled nonproducing wells. We find that prior to ABL renegotiations, high-leverage firms are more likely to complete wells on leases with neighboringundevelopedoil reserves rather than wells on previously developed oil leases.7These results are consistent with firms distorting their investments to enhance their collateral values, thereby mitigating lending frictions at renegotiation and expanding access to finance.
We conduct a set of tests to assess the stability of our results. First, given that our main analysis is based on a small sample of firms (69), we conduct a battery of tests that evaluate whether the results are driven by a few high-leverage firms in our sample. Second, we explore whether the results are robust to alternative definitions of leverage. Finally, we run placebo tests for alternative periods to assess the importance of the effect of contango on wells’ NPVs.
Overall, our findings highlight a novel debt-related investment distortion that arises at the intersection of high leverage, renegotiations (and related exposure to lending frictions), and the presence of collateral constraints for firms that rely heavily on ABL. This distortion is inconsistent with the traditional risk-shifting and debt-overhang channels. Instead, it speaks to overinvestment, and it arises in the absence of imminent technical or financial default. Prior literature argues that covenants and renegotiations lead to Pareto-improving reallocations of control rights and hence better investment outcomes (e.g., Chava and Roberts (2008), Roberts and Sufi (2009), Benmelech and Bergman (2009)) and thus argues that debt restrictions arevalue enhancing(Nini, Smith, and Sufi (2009), Denis and Wang (2014)) ex ante. We add to this literature by documenting that collateral constraints and debt renegotiations can lead to negative investment distortions ex post: High-leverage firms in our sample distort investment even before renegotiation and in the absence of covenant violations.
We also add to the expansive literature that explores the role collateral plays in alleviating financial frictions and hence enhancing firms’ access to capital. Benmelech and Bergman (2009) show that collateral allows firms to reduce the costs of external debt financing and expand debt capacity. Our study highlights previously unexplored hidden costs of collateral-based financing that are linked to the real investment activity of firms.
Finally, our findings relate to prior studies that explore the mechanisms that contribute to oil production being inelastic in the short run (Anderson, Kellogg, and Salant (2018)), which has important implications for the macroeconomic effects of oil price shocks (e.g., Kilian and Murphy (2012)). Existing studies attribute the inelastic supply to traditional oil production technology that is characterized by high-cost production adjustments (Anderson, Kellogg, and Salant (2018)). However, even with the emergence of new production technology (i.e., fracking) and its lower costs of production adjustment, firms’ oil production has remained inelastic (Lehn and Zhu (2016)). Our results suggest that the contractual nature of debt financing creates frictions that prevent downward adjustment in oil production when oil prices fall. We thus offer further support to the argument that the mounting debt of oil and gas firms is becoming an important factor in global oil price dynamics (Domanski et al. (2015)).
The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we provide institutional background and describe the data. In SectionII, we describe our empirical analysis and present the results. Finally, in SectionIII, we conclude.
article.I. Institutional Background and Data:
The shale oil industry offers an attractive setting in which to examine the relationship between leverage and investment decisions. The oil price futures curve provides firms (and econometricians) with the expected benefit associated with completing a well at any given date. The detailed high-frequency data on the precise timing of well completion allow us to evaluate both the investment outlays and the expected project benefits at the firm-project level. Granular information on well locations and the ability to observe timing of all oil extraction stages allows us to control for a variety of alternative factors linked to investment opportunities. Moreover, the ability to match the project-level data with firm characteristics allows us to link firms’ leverage and investment responses to changes in expected project value.
In this section, we provide more details on each individual component of our empirical setting. We first discuss the contango episode that we exploit. We then offer a detailed discussion of fracking projects in the oil and gas industry and describe the project-level data that we use. Finally, we discuss how the contango episode affected the NPVs of individual projects in our sample.
In this study, we exploit the unique evolution of spot and futures prices in the oil market between November 2014 and June 2015. In late 2014, abrupt changes in the oil price futures curve, due to OPEC's decision not to support oil prices, dramatically affected the expected returns from completing new oil wells early versus waiting to complete the wells. The decline started in late 2014 and was rapid, with spot prices falling below $50 per barrel in January of 2015. At that point, the oil market changed from traditional backwardation to severe contango. Panel A of Figure1shows that in January the two-year futures price exceeded the spot price by nearly 30% and the six-month futures prices exceeded the spot price by over 10%. This was a dramatic change from the backwardation in oil markets just two months prior. The 2015 futures curve exhibited substantial deviations from spot prices across all maturities. Panel B of Figure1compares the futures curve as of February 2015 to that as of September 2014.
The 2014 to 2015 contango episode was unprecedented as futures prices exceeded the spot price by more thanthree standard deviationsrelative to historical data. This episode was not only severe in terms of futures price deviation from the spot prices, but it was also abnormally long, extending through the rest of 2015. In April 2015, spot prices experienced a slight increase reaching $60, consistent with the upward price trajectory projected by futures prices as of the beginning of 2015. By mid-year, however, spot prices declined again and the market remained in strong contango through 2015 and into early 2016.
The severe contango episode of 2014 to 2015 played an important role in companies’ assessment of future expected cash flows from oil production operations. Futures prices are traditionally used by both academics and practitioners alike as a good estimate of expected future oil prices. Kellogg (2014) features this assumption prominently under risk-neutral traders and efficient aggregation of market information conditions. Consistent with the information content of oil futures in the postrecession era, most oil producers use futures prices in forming their expectations (Society of Petroleum Evaluation Engineers, (1995)).
Consider the well completion incentives under backwardation and contango. Under normal market backwardation, oil producers have incentives to accelerate oil production in the current period because spot prices exceed expected future prices. In contrast, during the severe contango episode, oil producers had a disincentive to initiate new production and bring new (fracking) wells online as the value of oil produced six months in the future was expected to be at least 11% higher than the value of oil produced in early 2015. More importantly, the pricing uncertainty associated with the decision to delay production could be fully eliminated. The oil producers could enter into a futures contract (at significantly higher prices due to contango) and store the oil underground while waiting to complete a well (at no or minimal marginal cost).8,9
Consistent with these arguments, we exploit the sharp shift to contango in December 2014/January 2015 as an exogenous shock to U.S. producers’ incentives to complete oil wells. Specifically, we expect the onset of contango to create a disincentive for firms to start production in favor of opening available reserves later.
In 2003, a surprise technological breakthrough combined horizontal drilling with hydraulic fracturing (i.e., fracking), enabling development of natural gas shale. In 2009, this innovation extended into oil development and dramatically reshaped the U.S. oil industry. Although prior to 2009 shale oil production contributed minimally to global oil supply, the technological change resulted in an increase in U.S. oil production from 5.4 million barrels per day in 2009 to 9.4 million barrels per day by the end of 2014. This increase represented 52% of the overall increase in oil production globally. Shale oil development has been one of the largest economic transformations that the U.S. economy has experienced, resulting in an aggregate increase in U.S. equity market capitalization of $2.5 trillion (Gilje, Ready, Roussanov (2016)).
By early 2014, 69 public oil and gas firms and more than 120 private firms were using fracking technology. By mid-2014 these companies were producing oil from over 200,000 oil wells across five states with shale oil fracking operations: Texas, Oklahoma, North Dakota, Colorado, and New Mexico.10In our analysis, we absorb the heterogeneity in investment opportunities via township geographic fixed effects.
Shale oil is located in geologic formations up to two miles below the earth's surface. To extract these reserves, firms first have to secure mineral rights from respective property owners. These oil leases allow energy companies to drill and then hydraulically frack shale formations to free oil from shale rock. Shale oil well drilling is somewhat unique relative to other types of oil extraction in that it requires two distinct project steps. First, the well must be spudded, or drilled. Second, the well must be completed, which involves hydraulic fracturing. To spud a well, the majority of firms in the industry rent a drilling rig from specialized service providers. It takes these service providers from three days to three weeks to drill onshore wells at an average cost of $2.97 million. The spudded well can sit idle until the company decides to complete or “frack” it.11The hydraulic fracturing process is separate from the drilling process and can occur at any time after initial drilling has been completed. The latter process also involves using a contractor that specializes in fracking (completing) wells. Firms that specialize in fracking include Halliburton, Baker Hughes, and Schlumberger. It typically takes two or three days to complete the fracturing process and costs about $3.5 million for an average well.
Production typically starts immediately after the well is completed, for a number of reasons. First, the completion specialist must test the well and report the initial production volume and oil quality measurements to the respective regulatory body (e.g., Texas Railroad Commission for the state of Texas). Once the well is fracked, disrupting the pressure in the well is prohibitively costly: The proppant (e.g., sand) used to frack the well begins to disintegrate immediately after a well is completed, which reduces the amount of oil that can be recovered from a well. Once a well is completed, production typically declines quickly: High initial pressure leads to high initial production, but as pressure is released from a well, production declines quickly month over month. In effect, each day oil is not pumped from a completed well is a day of lost production and cash flows. As a result, oil prices at the beginning of a well's productive life are critical in determining the economic return of a well. Panel A of Figure2depicts the declining after-tax cash flows based on the average production profile of 2,484 wells completed in North Dakota and the average January 2015 NYMEX futures curve.
The declining production schedule plays an important role during the period of super-contango. Panel B of Figure2illustrates the NPV of a decision to delay production by one to six months using the above production volume for a representative North Dakota well. This NPV calculation assumes a 10% discount rate, which is the rate that banks use to evaluate a firm's reserves and that firms use to report reserves to the SEC. The NPV calculation also relies on the average January 2015 NYMEX futures curve.12Figure2shows that delaying production improves individual project NPV. For example, a one-month delay in production in January 2015 increases a representative well's NPV from $2,435,718 to $2,559,735. The $124,017 increase constitutes a 4.8% increase in project NPV relative to immediate well completion.
The two-stage oil extraction process—spudding/drilling and completing/fracking—provides a number of empirical design benefits. First, we can isolate the decision to start production (i.e., complete a well) from that of drilling. Second, the ability to cap the well for an indefinite period of time after spudding, but before completion clearly separates two operational decisions. The costs of drilling become a sunk cost at the time the completion/production decision is made (Kellogg (2014)). Finally, the inability to delay production after the well has been completed and the completion date reporting requirement provides us with a precise completion decision date. We exploit all these features in constructing the sample of oil well projects used in our analysis.
Our sample consists of 3,557 wells developed by 69 publicly traded oil and gas firms. The well selection process is based on two stages of the oil development process. We start with the most comprehensive well drilling data set available, which is provided by RigData, Inc. RigData relies on public filings and relationships with a wide set of drilling contractors to precisely track the start of every shale well drilling operation in the United States. Our study focuses on shale oil drilling in Texas, North Dakota, Oklahoma, New Mexico, and Colorado, states in which 98% of U.S. shale oil drilling has taken place.
Although RigData provides the date of the first stage of well development (drilling/spudding), it does not provide the well completion (i.e., fracking) and production start date. We augment the information obtained from RigData by hand-collecting the completion date from regulatory filings on well completion collected by the oil industry regulatory bodies in major shale oil–producing states. These include the Texas Railroad Commission (form W-2), the Oklahoma Corporation Commission (form 1002A), the North Dakota Industrial Commission (form 6), New Mexico Department of Energy and Minerals (html web form), and the Colorado Oil and Gas Conservation Commission (html web form). We also cross check dates with completion information from fracfocus.org.
Our empirical strategy is to evaluate how contango affects individual firms’ completion decisions. Accordingly, we isolate the completion decision from the drilling decision by focusing on oil wells that were spudded before the onset of contango, that is, during the period September 2014 to November 2014. We then evaluate the completion decisions for these wells during the severe contango period from December 2014 to March 2015. To construct the control group, we augment this sample and consider wells spudded and potentially completed during the (more typical) backwardation period. Specifically, we include wells spudded in September 2013 to November 2013 and evaluate their completion decisions between December 2013 and March 2014 to those of benchmark firms. We maintain the same calendar months to eliminate potential explanations stemming from seasonal variation in oil production.
Our main variable of interest is the time between the well's spud date and completion date. Panel A of TableIshows that this variable exhibits significant heterogeneity in our sample. Some wells in our sample are completed as quickly as one month after the spud date, whereas others sit idle for more than two years before production commences. The median time between drilling and completion dates in our sample is four months.
To address investment opportunity based alternative explanations of our results, we exploit detailed information about the geographic location of the wells in our sample (land survey section township range or latitude and longitude) provided by the respective state regulatory bodies. These data allow us to control for well quality and investment opportunities as shale geologic qualities are similar over the six-mile by six-mile areas (i.e., townships) over which we conduct our comparisons. Finally, we supplement the well project drilling and completion data with additional well characteristics such as well completion costs and production volumes. We obtain these data from regulatory reports where available. For example, the North Dakota Industrial Commission provides the production schedules for a subset of wells, but similarly detailed data are not readily available from regulatory reports of other states in our sample.
We hand-match individual project-level data on well drilling and completion to a set of public oil and gas companies in Compustat following Gilje and Taillard (2016). Panel A of TableIsummarizes core financial characteristics of the oil and gas companies in our sample including the core variable of interest in this study—market leverage. Panel B splits our sample into high- and low-leverage firms, where we define all firms in the top 20% of the market leverage distribution as of the end of September 2014 as high-leverage firms and the remainder of the sample as low-leverage firms. Panel B of TableIshows that compared to low-leverage firms, high-leverage firms are smaller and are characterized by lower Tobin'sQ. However, high-leverage firms are comparable to low-leverage firms on other observable dimensions, namely, profitability, CAPEX, dividends, cash holdings, tangible assets, SGA (sales, general, and administrative expenses), and sales growth. Notably, high- and low-leverage firms within the ABL subsample are of much more comparable size and exhibit some differences in CAPEX. In empirical tests below we ensure that differences in size, CAPEX, and/or growth opportunities across high- and low-leverage firms are unlikely to explain our results.
Finally, we hand-collect detailed information about public firms’ credit lines and debt contracts from SEC filings provided by EDGAR. We focus on the size of the credit line offered by banks to firms in our sample, preset renegotiation dates, covenants, and collateral constraints imposed by the debt agreement. This information is collected for the years leading up to and including the contango episode (financial reports from 2013 to 2016).
article.II. Empirical Analysis and Results:
In this section, we first evaluate whether debt affects firms’ investment decisions by analyzing well completion behavior during contango. In SectionII.D, we assess a variety of potential concerns and alternative explanations for our results. Finally, in SectionII.E, we empirically evaluate two potential channels through which firms may benefit from accelerating investment—whether accelerating investment alleviates binding financial covenants and whether accelerating investment improves collateral, thereby mitigating information asymmetry or moral hazard and improving a firm's access to finance.
The analysis presented in this section compares the timing of well completion by high-and low-leverage firms (first difference) during the periods of contango and backwardation (second difference). This analysis allows us to validate the core underlying premise of our empirical design, namely that contango creates a disincentive for (all) oil and gas firms to complete wells and start production. Moreover, this analysis allows us to evaluate whether, during the contango episode, high-leverage firms complete wells quicker than low-leverage firms.
We focus on wells spudded between September and November 2014 and benchmark contango completions against the completion decisions of wells spudded between September and November 2013 when markets were in backwardation. The 2013 sample serves as our baseline period and allows us to difference out operator-specific differences in project completion time.
Panel A of TableIIpresents results of a univariate comparison of the time to completion decision across high- and low-leverage firms during periods of contango and backwardation. We find that low-leverage oil producers, those in the bottom four quintiles of the leverage distribution, behave as expected. They delay well completion during super-contango by about one month compared to well completion in 2013. The extension length is statistically and economically significant, as it constitutes 16% to 36% of the average completion time (depending on the low-leverage quintile). In contrast, high-leverage (top quintile) producers do not delay production. The lack of adjustment to completion time for high-leverage firms is consistent with the notion that debt distorts firms’ real investments.
One can argue that the differences in completion times can be driven by other unobserved heterogeneity, such as differences in investment projects or growth opportunities, rather than financial leverage. High-leverage firms may have access to or choose to complete wells that require less capital or are more/less productive. TableIshows that high- and low-leverage firms differ in terms of their Tobin'sQ. Existing literature documents that investment opportunities could be linked to leverage (Lang, Ofek, and Stulz (1996), Ahn, Denis, and Denis (2006))—firms with bad investment opportunities could also have high leverage. If this is the case, the incentives of high-leverage firms to delay production might be weaker than what our analysis captures.
Panel A of TableIIIconfirms that high- and low-leverage firms are indeed characterized by different investment opportunities at the project (well) level. The results show that high-leverage firms’ wells are characterized by lower production volumes and lower completion CAPEX. To address this concern, we exploit the fact that well quality (production capacity and costs of completion) is driven by shale reservoir characteristics, which we can control for using a set of highly granular six-mile by six-mile (township) geographic fixed effects.
Panel B of TableIIIillustrates the efficacy of six-mile by six-mile geographic fixed effects. The results document that wells located at most within 8.5 miles (62+62)1/2of each other (within one township) do not exhibit dramatic differences in completion costs and production volume. We find that while the subsamples of high- and low-leverage firms exhibit economically significant differences in well costs and production, these differences are not statistically or economically different in a regression setting that incorporates fine geographic fixed effects. The highly granular geography fixed effects captures the heterogeneity in investment opportunities across high- and low-leverage firms.
Panel A of TableIVreports the results, where the dependent variable is the number of months to completion. We find that in contango, firms in all but the top quintile of the leverage distribution delay well completion by approximately one month. For example, column (1) demonstrates this nonlinear dependence of wait times during contango on leverage and shows that firms in the top leverage quintile pull completion forward relative to their less-constrained peers. Furthermore, in column (3) we find that the coefficient onContinuousLeveragei×Contangotis negative, though not statistically significant. The difference in nonlinear and linear results is consistent with the nonlinear effect of other leverage distortions (i.e., risk shifting and debt overhang).
Since all but one of the 13 firms in the top leverage quintile have ABL and are exposed to prescheduled renegotiations, whereas only about half of the firms in the other leverage quintiles have ABL, one may argue that the effect we document could be driven by asset-based borrowing rather than leverage. To address this concern, in Panel B of TablesIIandIV, we repeat our empirical experiment after restricting our sample to firms with ABL. In our sample, 45 of 69 firms have such debt agreements. The results show that compared to low-leverage firms with ABL, high-leverage firms with ABL accelerate well completion by one month during contango. This evidence suggests that the documented investment distortion cannot be attributed to ABL alone. We therefore conclude that leverage plays an important role in the acceleration of well completions.14
To assess explanations based on observed heterogeneity distinct from leverage (recall TableI), we augment the analysis presented in TableIVby adding interaction terms between the contango dummy and the other firm-level control variables measured prior to contango (i.e., as of September 2014). TableVshows that heterogeneity in firm size, profitability, CAPEX, and Tobin'sQis unlikely to account for our results. Specifically, Panel A shows that our core inferences do not change after we control for the respective interaction terms. However, Panel B shows that even if we allow these firm characteristics to affect well completion decisions in a nonlinear fashion (by using quintile dummies) high-leverage firms continue to accelerate well completion by one month relative to their low-leverage industry peers. Once again, we find that leverage has both economically and statistically significant effects on well completion decisions in contango irrespective of the statistical approach underlying the standard errors.
Overall, the lack of a completion time response for high-leverage firms is consistent with the view that debt distorts firms’ investment decisions in the presence of credit market frictions. Based on the representative well-level cash flows presented in Figure2, we estimate that by not expanding time to completion by one month, high-leverage firms are forgoing 4.8% of project NPV or $124,017 per project. This number is economically significant. Considering the number of projects available for completion to each high-leverage firm during contango, acceleration of project completion by one month has the potential to create a deadweight loss of 1.2% of equity value for high-leverage firms.15
In this section, we evaluate completion decisions of high- and low-leverage firms around ABL renegotiations that by definition expose firms to credit frictions. In addition to lending support to our conclusions above, this analysis allows us to evaluate whether lending frictions play an important role in firms’ decisions to accelerate oil production. Since the ABL renegotiation dates are prescheduled about every six months, these dates can be considered exogenous to the onset of contango and related changes in firms’ financial conditions. We exploit the spring 2015 debt renegotiations staggered over February, March, April, and May of 2015 and compare the well completion decisions of high- versus low-leverage firms (first difference) around debt renegotiation dates (second difference).
In this setting, evaluating the time to completion is uninformative because for wells drilled over the September through November 2014 period, the time to completion increases purely mechanically from before renegotiation to after renegotiation. Consequently, we adapt our dependent variable such that it captures the start of production decisions in an on/off fashion. Specifically, our dependent variableWellStartsProductionijttakes the value of 1 if welljof firmiis completed (turned on from idle to producing) in montht, and 0 in all other months (both before the completion month and following the completion month). As before, the unit of observation is welljof firmiin montht. By averaging the dependent variable within each firmiand each montht, one obtains the share of firmi’s ex ante spudded wells that started producing in montht. If debt renegotiations intensify the distortive effects of leverage on investment decisions, high-leverage firms should complete a higher percentage of wells prior to debt renegotiations as compared to the completion decisions of low-leverage firms.
TableVIreports results of univariate analysis on completion decisions around renegotiation dates for high- and low-leverage firms. The evidence is consistent with distortive effects of high leverage. In particular, the estimate of 0.22 at time (t− 1) for high-leverage firms suggests that one month prior to renegotiation, high-leverage firms complete 22% of the wells they had spudded in the fall of 2014. This number drops to 9% in the month after renegotiation, a change that is both economically and statistically significant. To put this result into perspective, high-leverage firms complete 145% more wells in the month prior to renegotiation than the month of or the month after renegotiation (0.22/0.09−1). In contrast, low-leverage firms exhibit a much smaller change in well-completion percentage around debt renegotiation dates, from a 12% rate of completion prior to renegotiation to a 7% rate of completion after renegotiation.
In the equation above,WellStartsProductionijmtis a dummy variable equal to 1 if welljof firmistarts production in monthtand this month ismmonths removed from the month of credit agreement renegotiation, and 0 otherwise. For example, if firmihas a credit renegotiation in March of 2015 and welljis completed in April 2015, thenWellStartsProductionijmApril=1andWellStartsProductionijmMarch=0. Firm-level fixed effects control for heterogeneity in firm characteristics. Since we limit attention to the behavior of oil and gas firm behavior over a six-month span, these fixed effects control for a wide range of unobservable firm characteristics, such as firms’ size, profitability, Tobin'sQ, and CAPEX. Geographic fixed effects control for heterogeneity in project quality and production potential. Month fixed effects control for changes in economic conditions, including changes in oil prices and severity of contango. The control group for the baseline results reported in specifications (1) to (3) of TableVIIcomprises all low-leverage firms and high-leverage firms that do not have a prescheduled renegotiation during the spring of 2015. The coefficient of interest isβm. If debt renegotiations intensify the distortive effects of leverage on investment decisions, we expect firms to complete more wells just prior to debt renegotiations (m =−1 orm =−2) than after the renegotiation decisions (m =0 orm =1).
The regression results support the conclusions implied by the univariate analysis reported in TableVI. For example, column (2) of TableVIIsuggests that between the month prior to and the month of renegotiation, high-leverage firms exhibit an 11.3% drop in the rate of well completion (the coefficient drops from −0.022 to −0.135) as compared to non-ABL firms and other high-leverage firms not going through renegotiation in a given month. This effect is similar in magnitude to the 0.13 change in completion rate documented in TableVI. In contrast, the number of new wells being brought on line by low-leverage firms (column (3) of TableVII) does not vary across debt renegotiation dates in a statistically or economically significant way (the coefficient changes from −0.007 inm =−1 to −0.006 inm =0).
In column (4) of TableVII, we rerun the analysis above based solely on the sample of high-leverage firms’ wells. In this setting high-leverage firms not going through renegotiations provide the counterfactual for high-leverage firms facing renegotiations (the control group consists of other high-leverage firms that have not (yet) been treated). In column (5), we run an identical analysis for low-leverage ABL firms. The results suggest that even in the presence of lending frictions, low-leverage firms do not distort investments. However, lending frictions do play an important role in inducing high-leverage firms to accelerate well completion. This evidence is consistent with high leverage leading to distortion only in the presence of lending frictions—in our setting, induced by renegotiations (Stein (2003)). This pattern is important to take into account in identifying potential economic mechanisms driving our results.
In this section, we discuss various tests that we conduct to assess the sensitivity of our results to different specification assumptions. We start by examining whether our results are sensitive to alternative definitions of leverage: (i) book leverage calculated as the book value of debt normalized by the total book value of debt plus the book value of equity; and (ii) the (reverse) interest coverage ratio, calculated as the ratio of interest expense divided by EBITDA. Both of these variables are measured as of September 2014. The interest coverage ratio is reversed to ensure comparability of the results (highest quintile comparable to high-leverage-quintile firms). Internet Appendix TablesIA.ItoIA.IVreport results consistent with those in TablesII, IV,VI, andVII: high-leverage firms accelerate well completion by about one month relative to low-leverage firms, with the investment distortion more pronounced in the month prior to ABL renegotiation.
Another concern that arises in studies that examine the effect of leverage on firms’ financial decisions is that leverage may not be randomly distributed. In particular, one may argue that our results are due to an omitted variable that affects both debt and completion decisions (e.g., firm size or investment opportunities) or to firms choosing debt based on their business strategy (reverse causality), which would lead to alternative interpretations of our results. Although we do not offer a viable instrument for firm leverage, several pieces of evidence support a debt-based interpretation over the view that our results are due to omitted variable bias or reverse causality.
First, our identification relies on the contango episode being unanticipated by both firms and the market. The 2014 to 2015 contango episode was exceedingly rare in that the difference between the futures price and the spot price exceeded its average by over three standard deviations. To determine whether our results are indeed due to contango, and not a manifestation of a long-term trend whereby high-leverage firms accelerate well completion over time, we conduct Placebo tests. Internet Appendix TableIA.Vreports the results. This analysis is similar to that in TableIV, but instead of comparing firm completion behavior around the end of the 2014 contango episode, we look at placebo years. Internet Appendix TableIA.Vreports the results. In Panel A (B), we compare the completion time of wells spudded between September and November of 2013 (2012) and wells spudded between September and November of 2012 (2011). The results show no significant discernable differences in well completion along the time dimension (equivalent to a contango dummy) or across high- and low-leverage firms. These results lend further support to our empirical design. Furthermore, these results make it more challenging to argue that firms could have anticipated the severe contango of 2014 and chosen their leverage accordingly, that is, they do not support explanations based on reverse causality.
Second, the evidence of well completion activity around debt renegotiations (where we control for firm fixed effects) also allows us to further mitigate concerns about unobserved heterogeneity. It is difficult to argue that changes in completion behavior around debt renegotiation dates can be attributed to economic channels that exclude debt-related forces. It is also difficult to come up with an economic explanation for the differential completion activity of high- and low-leverage firms pre- and post-renegotiation after controlling for firm-level fixed effects that is entirely unrelated to leverage, TableVIshows that high-leverage firms complete more wells than low-leverage firms pre-renegotiation (22% versus 12%) but have similar completion rates post-renegotiation (9% versus 7%).
One might also be concerned that the results we document above are due to the contemporaneous rapid decline in oil prices rather than firm leverage or contango. For example, a decrease in spot oil prices might lead to significant changes in asset values that vary across high- and low-leverage firms (see Gilje and Taillard (2016)). Several pieces of evidence suggest that an oil price change itself does not fundamentally affect the interpretation of our results. First, our main tests are based on comparisons of investment decisions in thesame monthin assets ofsimilar qualitylocated in thesame townshipbut owned by firms withdifferent leverage. Second, the key advantage of focusing on contango is that it creates incentives to delay the completion of projects even when oil prices are low.
Finally, while our empirical setting offers a unique opportunity to evaluate firm investment decisions at the project level, our analysis is based on a small sample of firms—only 69 public oil and gas firms are included in our analysis. One may therefore be concerned that the results we document are driven by a few (of the 13) high-leverage firms accelerating completion for reasons unrelated to debt. To assess this concern, we conduct two analyses. First, in TableVIIIwe evaluate whether the results presented in TableIVare driven by a single high-leverage firm in our sample. TableVIIIprovides relevant statistics on each high-leverage firm considered separately as well as the interaction coefficients corresponding to the specification in TableIV, column (2) after dropping the given high-leverage firm from the sample. The coefficients of interest fall in the range [−1.18 to −0.86] and remain statistically significant.
In TableIX, we examine whether excluding up to the three highest leverage firms and redefining the leverage quintiles affects the results in TableIV. We find that while the magnitudes of the core coefficient of interest is relatively stable within the sample of ABL firms (i.e., those exposed to credit renegotiations and related lending frictions), the magnitude and the statistical significance of the core coefficients decline when we consider both the ABL and non-ABL firms jointly. Specifically, the robustness tests for the results in Panel A of TableIV(reported in Panels A, B, and C of TableIX) show significant compression in the magnitude of the core coefficient, as it reaches −0.6 at its lowest point versus −1.0 in TableIV. In contrast, in Panel B of TableIX, where we focus on ABL firms, we find that the core coefficient's magnitude and statistical significance are more stable, with the coefficient varying between −1.005 and −1.007. These results again highlight the importance of renegotiations as an economic mechanism underlying the documented overinvestment.16
The results presented in TablesIItoVIIare consistent with high leverage distorting firms’ investment decisions, particularly so around renegotiations. In this section, we consider the economic channels underlying the effect of debt and renegotiations on sample firms’ investment decisions.
The rationale for delaying completion during contango is unambiguous, and any finance professional should see the value that accelerating production leaves on the table. Thus, barring lending frictions firms should be able to renegotiate their credit agreements in a way that allows them to delay production and capture the additional value under contango (e.g., through rent sharing). This argument suggests that our results may be driven by lending frictions that stem from asymmetric information and moral hazard (Aghion and Bolton (1992); Stulz and Johnson (1985); Hart and Moore (1995, 1998)). On the moral hazard side, lenders may perceive a delay in well completion as an attempt by managers to divert funds to bad projects, siphon funds off to pay shareholders/management, invest in higher risk projects, or otherwise misappropriate funds (Berger and Udell (1990); John, Lynch, and Puri (2003); Jiménez, Salas, and Saurina (2006)). On the asymmetric information side, lenders may interpret a delay in well completion as an attempt to misrepresent the value of nonproducing reserves to secure continued access to (asset-based) finance.
Covenants and collateral are prominently featured in prior literature as mechanisms that can mitigate such lending frictions ex ante (at the point of debt renegotiations). Indeed, both covenants and collateral increase firms’ access to debt finance and lower the cost of capital (Chava and Roberts (2008); Benmelech and Bergman (2009)). However, both covenants and collateral also increase frictions ex post, particularly for high-leverage firms. Covenant violations lead to a decrease in firm investments (Chava and Roberts (2008); Nini, Smith, and Sufi (2009)) and reduce the availability of bank lines of credit (Sufi (2009)). A decrease in collateral value similarly leads to a reduction in firm investment (Chaney, Sraer, and Thesmar (2012)).
Based on the above discussion, in this section we evaluate whether our results can be explained by frictions related to two aspects of oil and gas firms’ access to debt finance, namely (i) binding covenants and corresponding cash flow needs, and (ii) collateral constraints.
First, we examine whether the decision to complete wells early is driven by binding covenants and related cash flow needs. To avoid postcovenant–violation investment distortions (Chava and Roberts (2008)) firms accelerate cash flows and manage earnings (e.g., Dichev and Skinner (2002)). One may argue that when facing binding covenants, firms distort their real decisions in an attempt to accelerate cash flows. For example, a revenue shortfall associated with low oil prices in late 2014, might be felt more acutely by high-leverage firms characterized by higher interest payments and potentially binding covenant restrictions. This pressure may induce the high-leverage firms to accelerate production and secure access to funds necessary to avoid real or technical default.
To evaluate this conjecture, we analyze the covenant and cash flow characteristics of the firms in our sample. We find that the high-leverage firms in our sample do not face binding covenant constraints. As of March 2015, these firms are characterized by an average interest coverage ratio of 3.18 and an average current ratio (current assets/current liabilities) of 2.464. These suggest that during our sample period, high-leverage firms generate sufficient cash flows to cover interest payments. In addition, we collect detailed data on credit limits and find that high-leverage firms have spare debt capacity, as they use on average only 37.3% of their credit facilities. Taken together, these results suggest that while high-leverage firms may not have the strongest balance sheets, they are not vulnerable to imminent default, nor do they face an impending liquidity crunch at the time of credit renegotiation.
A careful analysis of cash flows associated with a representative well further works against a liquidity/cash flow–based explanation of our results. Acceleration of well completion by an average of one month does indeed accelerate the cash in-flows associated with a producing well (average EBITDA = $0.35 million in the first month of production). However, early well completion also accelerates $3.5 million CAPEX. Thus, on balance, early well completion should negatively affect immediate cash availability. Acceleration of well completion should also negatively affect a firm's cash flows over the medium term, as the early-completed well generates less revenue as a result of lower oil prices at the peak of the well's operational capacity but requires the same completion CAPEX. These results suggest that early well completion is highly unlikely to yield high-leverage firms a better cash position (e.g., sufficient to cover interest payments). Furthermore, the pattern of project-level cash flow almost mechanically rules out covenant-based explanations. Given that financial covenant metrics are typically backward-looking (e.g., current debt to trailing 12-month EBITDA), expending capital (funded by debt on the margin) to complete a well would adversely affect a firm's near-term financial covenant ratios.
Overall, the short-term nature of the decision we evaluate, the cash flow profile of a representative well, and the available covenant data allow us to reject the view that binding financial covenants drive our results.
The second economic channel that we consider stems from the observation that even in the absence of binding (backward-looking) covenants, high-leverage firms have a more vested interest in ensuring continued access to finance for future investment. For example, one-year-ahead investment plans reported by firms in their 2014 10Ks constitute on average 106% of their year-end cash flows and thus require expanded access to credit. For ABL firms that drive our core results, well completions affect not only their cash flows but also the value of pledgeable assets that can be used to secure/expand their access to finance. Both asymmetric information and moral hazard lending frictions (at the point of renegotiation) create incentives for firms to increase collateral at the expense of lower project-level NPVs. Ultimately, equity holders could be likely to forgo project-level returns in exchange for greater credit availability. This effect is expected to be more pronounced for riskier firms that depend more on asset-based borrowing (Berger and Udell (1990)).
To evaluate this conjecture, we exploit the approach that banks use to determine the extent of credit to firms in the oil and gas industry. Specifically, for ABL firms in our sample, the expected value of collateral determines the “borrowing base,” that is, the upper limit on lines of credit established to finance oil exploration and production operations. Given the information asymmetry between firms and banks about the quality of oil reserves, lenders apply different haircuts to expected cash flow values of developed and undeveloped wells, as well as to nonproducing reserves that are geographically proximate, to versus far from, producing reserves.
Figure3shows the “risk-adjustment factors” that the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) recommends banks use in calculating the NPVs of different types of reserves when determining the borrowing base.17The OCC guidance suggests that the borrowing base be adjusted up when (i) oil prices increase, (ii) new wells are completed and moved from proved undeveloped to proved producing reserves, (iii) higher production volume wells are completed, and (iv) newly completed wells reduce information asymmetry about the value of other (typically neighboring) nonproducing reserves. Apart from directly affecting the collateral value of completed wells, production decisions can increase collateral values assigned to nonproducing and undeveloped resources.
The above discussion suggests that spudded wells exhibit significant heterogeneity in their ability to affect the borrowing base once completed, heterogeneity that stems from production capacity and in their ability to reduce the information asymmetry between firms and banks with respect to the value of neighboring nonproducing reserves. For example, if a firm completes a well on a lease with existing producing wells (multi-well lease), it receives collateral credit for the newly added production volume, but well completion does not offer additional information about neighboring (already producing) reserves. In contrast, if the same firm completes the first well on an oil lease (single-well lease), it not only receives collateral credit for the newly added production volume, but it also realizes the benefits from reduced information asymmetry about the quality of neighboring nonproducing reserves. Thus, ceteris paribus, wells on single-well leases have higher collateral impact. SectionIof the Internet Appendix provides a numerical example that compares the collateral effect of completing a representative well on single- versus multi-well leases.
We explore the two margins of collateral adjustment above in our empirical tests. First, we compare production volume of wells completed right before debt renegotiation to that of wells completed right after debt renegotiation. Univariate analysis presented in TableXshows that wells completed by high-leverage firms just prior to credit renegotiation have higher initial production volume than wells completed after renegotiation. Specifically, prior to debt renegotiation, firms initiate production on wells that produce 417 barrels per day, versus 292 barrels per day right after debt renegotiation.
Second, we exploit the heterogeneous effect of single- and multi-well lease completions on collateral values. In TableXI, we split our sample of wells based on these two types of well leases and compare companies’ completion behavior within each category. We find that high-leverage ABL firms accelerate completion on high-collateral-impact single-well leases prior to renegotiations. This effect is economically and statistically absent among completions on low-collateral-impact multi-well leases. TableXIIconfirms these results in a regression setting similar to that reported in TableVII. Column (1) shows that high-leverage ABL firms’ rate of high-collateral-impact well completion declines by 21% after debt renegotiation month (0), which points to and indicates acceleration of completion prior to renegotiations, whereas in column (4) we find that low-collateral-impact well completion behavior around the renegotiation date is fairly uniform. These results continue to hold when we run the renegotiation-focused DiD analysis separately for high- and low-leverage ABL firms (columns (2) to (3) and (5) to (6)).
In a final analysis, we address the concern that single-well lease and multi-well leases could differ on dimensions beyond their impact on collateral values. For example, if single-well leases are on average higher risk, higher reward projects, then our evidence may be indicative of risk-shifting rather than a collateral-based mechanism. Using production data on a broad set of wells across public and private companies, we verify that on average single-well leases exhibit both lower initial production and a lower standard deviation of production realizations. This evidence is not consistent with risk-shifting but rather is congruent with arguments presented by Gilje (2016).18
Taken together, the evidence above indicates that the ability of collateral to mitigate credit frictions helps drive our main results. In particular, the results suggest that in making completion decisions during contango, high-leverage ABL firms are driven to expand their access to finance.
article.III. Discussion and Conclusion:
In this paper, we document a new debt-related investment distortion and link this distortion to firms’ incentives to create collateral. Our results suggest that by increasing collateral values, high-leverage firms (dependent on asset-based loans) reduce financing frictions at debt renegotiations. Specifically, we show that during the 2014/2015 contango episode, high-leverage firms completed wells and started production earlier than low-leverage firms. This acceleration of production adversely affected the NPV of oil projects, which, according to our estimates, was equivalent to a deadweight loss of about 1.2% of high-leverage firms’ equity value. The prevalence of such adverse investment decisions was particularly pronounced prior to (prescheduled) debt renegotiations—high-leverage firms completed 145% more wells in the month preceding renegotiations than in the month of and after renegotiations (when their rate of well completion was equal to that of low-leverage firms). These results suggest that high-leverage firms, which face greater lending frictions, resort to accumulating collateral to secure and/or expand their access to finance.
The literature has long argued that riskier firms depend more on secured financing (Berger and Udell (1990)). Collateral plays a more important role in high-leverage firms’ cost and access to debt financing as compared to low-leverage firms (Benmelech and Bergman (2009)). Low-leverage firms can potentially substitute away from asset-based financing to other forms of financing. We find that high-leverage firms forgo project-level NPV to boost collateral values prior to renegotiations. Our evidence therefore suggests that high-leverage firms are willing to pay (in the form of reduced project NPV) to ensure continued access to cheaper and more abundant credit.
Our study reveals previously undocumented hidden costs of collateral-based financing. More than 70% of commercial and industrial loans are issued on a secured basis (Berger and Udel (1990)). Even publicly traded firms rely on bank debt and exploit secured credit to a significant extent (Rauh and Sufi (2010)). Secured or not, debt contracts are frequently renegotiated even in the absence of distress or default (Denis and Wang (2014); Roberts and Sufi (2009)). These renegotiations result in significant changes to contract terms such as amounts, maturity, and pricing (Roberts and Sufi (2009)). Roberts and Sufi (2009) estimate that close to 90% of debt contracts are renegotiated before the stated maturity. Our results therefore provide important implications for the wide set of firms that have asset-based debt and are subject to renegotiation and refinancing-related lending frictions.
Our findings also help shed light on the inelastic short-run oil production puzzle (Anderson, Kellogg, and Salant (2018)) that has implications for the macroeconomic effects of oil price shocks (e.g., Kilian and Murphy (2012)). Prior studies attribute inelastic oil supply to traditional oil production technology, which is characterized by high-cost production adjustments. However, even with the emergence of new production technology (i.e., fracking) that has relatively low production adjustment costs, firms’ oil production has remained inelastic. Our results suggest that the contractual nature of debt financing creates frictions that prevent downward adjustments in oil production when oil prices fall. Our results therefore offer further support to the argument that oil and gas firms’ mounting debt is becoming an important factor in global oil price dynamics.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
